coredata.dc:title: Ag-TiO2@Pd/C nanocomposites for efficient degradation of Reactive Red 120 dye and ofloxacin antibiotic under UV and solar light and its antimicrobial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Ravikumar, S.
Mani, Durai
Khan, Mohammad Rizwan

Sylvestre, S.

coredata.dc:description: Photodegradation technique is also being employed with naturally available solar energy for treating toxic pollutants to protect environment. In order to achieve the effective degradation of pollutants, the photo-induced charge separation should be in suitable levels. The stability of the catalyst plays an important role in photodegradation. To achieve the above two concepts successfully, the Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C nanocomposites were fabricated by adding different percentages of Pd/C with respect to Ag-TiO2. The formation of the nanocomposites has been exclusively described by different characterization techniques. The prepared nanocomposites have been effectively tested with Reactive Red 120 (RR 120) under direct solar & UV-A light and their performances were explored. In this work, the test parameters such as photocatalyst reusability test and different dye pH have been discussed. Further, the catalyst degraded the antibiotic ofloxacin (OFL), which is being used for the treatment of a number of bacterial infections and the degradation mechanism is proposed below. GC-MS analysis was performed, based on the retention time, molecular ion peaks and intermediates were analyzed and proposed suitable degradation pathways for RR 120 and ofloxacin drug mineralization by Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C nanocomposite. In addition, the anti-microbial activity of synthesized nanocomposites was investigated against twenty pathogens such as Gram-positive Cocci (GBC) (2), Gram-positive Bacilli (GPB) (1), Gram negative Bacilli (GNB) (15) and fungal (2).

head.ce:title: Ag-TiO2@Pd/C nanocomposites for efficient degradation of Reactive Red 120 dye and ofloxacin antibiotic under UV and solar light and its antimicrobial activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodegradation technique is also being employed with naturally available solar energy for treating toxic pollutants to protect environment. In order to achieve the effective degradation of pollutants, the photo-induced charge separation should be in suitable levels. The stability of the catalyst plays an important role in photodegradation. To achieve the above two concepts successfully, the Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C nanocomposites were fabricated by adding different percentages of Pd/C with respect to Ag-TiO2. The formation of the nanocomposites has been exclusively described by different characterization techniques. The prepared nanocomposites have been effectively tested with Reactive Red 120 (RR 120) under direct solar & UV-A light and their performances were explored. In this work, the test parameters such as photocatalyst reusability test and different dye pH have been discussed. Further, the catalyst degraded the antibiotic ofloxacin (OFL), which is being used for the treatment of a number of bacterial infections and the degradation mechanism is proposed below. GC-MS analysis was performed, based on the retention time, molecular ion peaks and intermediates were analyzed and proposed suitable degradation pathways for RR 120 and ofloxacin drug mineralization by Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C nanocomposite. In addition, the anti-microbial activity of synthesized nanocomposites was investigated against twenty pathogens such as Gram-positive Cocci (GBC) (2), Gram-positive Bacilli (GPB) (1), Gram negative Bacilli (GNB) (15) and fungal (2).

ce:section.ce:para: Now a days, many reports reveal that there are thousands of harmful dyes manufactured from various chemical industries for the human needs at different levels and ultimately, they find their way into different water streams through the effluents discharged from dyeing and printing industries, textile, leather, paper and ink industries [1–4]. These dyes are prepared from the following organic components such as coal, tar, benzene, naphthalene, anthracene, toluene, xylenes etc., and these dye effluents provide color to water streams. They are untraceable, toxic and are suspected to be carcinogenic in nature. These organic dyes in water impart colors even when present in trace quantities, hence, affects the aquatic life and create non curable problems against the green environment [5–7].

Other major important environmental pollutants are pharmaceutical waste that is released by the pharma industries. They are easily contaminating the drinking water stream and largely affect the livelihood of both human and animals thus create pollution for aquatic biota [8]. However, unwanted high consumption of antibiotic and other harmful drugs may lead to overactive immune system, that leads to many autoimmune disorders. Generally, these molecules are very complex in structure and hence, it is very difficult to degrade it completely from the wastewater streams by the usual existing techniques [9]. To safeguard the human beings and marine environment from the toxic pollutant; these must be treated with a proper treatment option.

In the wastewater treatment applications, there are challenges faced in for degrading a greater number of toxic molecules by general methods like physical, biological and chemical alone. Hence, an Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) technique is required to overcome the above methods by producing a superoxide radicals (radical O2 •–) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH radical). These powerful oxidizing agents are used to break the organic pollutant more vigorously and this technique has been attained somewhat satisfactory results. The biggest drawback of the AOPs is its cost for implementation in the wastewater treatment. Now days, photocatalysis using semiconductor materials have been a promising technology to give perfect solution for treating wastewater pollutant [10]. TiO2 photocatalyst has been considered to be a favorable candidate for wastewater treatment applications due its low cost, reusability, cheap availability, non-toxicity and chemical stability. However, its wide band gap energy (3.0–3.2 eV) has promoted the material to be a best catalyst for environmental remediation applications. The photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 is rapidly affected by fast recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, which reduces the photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, various efforts have been made to improve the efficient charge separation including coupling of metals/noble metals, various semiconductors oxides, sulfides, halides, surface modification and grafting of organic compounds [11–20].

In the field of photocatalysis, especially for toxic chemical degradation, the design of the materials (semiconductors) has attracted plentiful research attentions to accomplish good performance of the degradation process [21–23]. Silver modified TiO2 thin film was fabricated, characterized and used for methylene blue (MB) degradation under visible light [24]. Recently, May-Lozano et al., [25] reported a ternary system Ag modified TiO2 along with graphene oxide for effective degradation of dyes. They reported that Ag and graphene oxide modification on TiO2 effectively enhances the catalytic activity of the TiO2. The synthesis of nanocomposite is one the great interests of researchers since it has lot of applications in various filed. Bio application of the nanomaterials is also one of the current topics of research [26–32]. Catalytic inactivation of bacteria using Pd-modified titania was reported [33]. Authors have prepared Pd and Pt modified TiO2 and effectively utilized for photocatalytic sterilization of Escherichia coli in water. Pd/TiO2 was found to work significantly faster than Pt/TiO2, and was also active in the absence of light [33].

Very recently, Lizundia et al., [34] reported synergistic effects between lignin and various metal nanoparticles with poly(L‑lactide) bio nanocomposites. Authors have used Ag, Ti, W and Fe based metal oxides containing lignin solvent cast films of PLLA-based nanocomposites for inactivation of various bacteria. Noble metals, especially Ag nanoparticles (NPs) play a significant role in bio-application by destroying the cell walls [35,36]. Composites containing noble metals would have a good synergistic effect and has attracted extensive interest in the field of energy and environmental research. In this present work, the synergistic effect between Ag and Pd@C on TiO2 has been investigated towards photocatalytic degradations of toxic dye RR 120 and ofloxacin drug using UV and solar light under various conditions. In addition, the anti-microbial activity of synthesized nanocomposites was investigated against twenty pathogens. The utilization of the prepared nanocomposites towards effective environmental remediation are discussed in details.

Titanium isopropoxide (TiiP), Pd/C (10 wt% of ‘Pd’), ofloxacin (OFL) antibiotic were obtained from sigma Aldrich. The azo dye Reactive Red 120 (RR 120) (Balaji Color Company, Chennai) and Gelatin (C102H151O39N31, Sino pharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) were used as received. Experimental solutions were prepared using distilled water. The instrumentation, photocatalytic and GC-MS procedures are given in Supporting Informations (see Supporting Information).

The schematic representation of Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C composite synthesis is given in Supporting Information (Scheme S1). Initially, sol-gel method was adopted for the synthesis of Ag-TiO2. About 0.1182 g of AgNO3 was dissolved in 5 mL of DI water (A). A viscous solution B was obtained by dissolving 0.1 g of gelatin in 5 mL of hot water, and this gelatin was used as template. Solutions A and B were added simultaneously to solution C containing 10 mL TiiP in 90 mL of 2-propanol under vigorous stirring condition. The stirring was continued until next day (24 h), the obtained gel was filtered followed by washing with mixture of 1:1 water/ethanol solution. After air dried in a hot air oven at 80 °C for 5 h, the obtained powder was calcined at 450 °C in a furnace for 5 h. The Ag-TiO2 (3 wt% Ag with respect to TiO2) powder was collected for the next process. The TiO2 was prepared without addition of solution A.

Simple solid-state method was adopted for the synthesis of Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C composites. Two separate alcoholic (2-propanol) dispersions were prepared with Ag-TiO2 (1.94 g in 40 mL) and Pd/C (0.06 g in 10 mL) under sonication, and two were mixed under stirring condition, the stirring continued until next day (24 h). The formed composite was filtered and washed with mixture of ethanol/water (1:1), and air dried in a hot air oven at 115 °C for 5 h. This composite contains 3 wt% of Pd/C. The same procedure was adopted for the synthesis of 1 and 5 wt% of Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C with respective amount of Pd/C. The TiO2 @Pd/C (3 wt%) was prepared using the same procedure with TiO2.

The anti-microbial activity was employed against twenty (bacterial (eighteen) and fungi (two)) pathogens. The bacteria employed for the screening were Gram positive cocci (Micrococcus sp., Staphylococcus aureus), Gram positive bacilli (Bacillus subtilis), Gram negative bacilli (Acinetobacter baumanii, Enterobacter sp., Escherichia coli, Flavobacterium sp., Klebsiella pneumoniae, Proteus mirabilis, Proteus vulgaris, Providentia sp., Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Vibrio cholerae Salmonella typhimurium, Salmonella paratyphi A, Salmonella paratyphi B, Serratia marcescens, and Salmonella typhi) and two fungal pathogens such as Candida albicans, Cryptococcus neoformans. All the bacterial assays were performed in Mueller-Hinton (MH) media and all the assays using fungus were performed in Yeast Malt (YM) media. Stock solution of nanoparticle was prepared dispersing in 1 mL sterile distilled water according to each concentration (0.78, 1.56, 3.12, 6.25 and 12.5 µg/mL) requirement for preparing 20 mL of Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) plates. After pouring 20 mL of MHA into each Petri plate and appropriate concentrations (0.78, 1.56, 3.12, 6.25 and 12.5 µg/mL) of nanoparticles were pipetted into the plate using 1 mL pipette. The molten MHA was mixed with the nanoparticles solution by swirling the plate and allowed to solidify. MHA plates without nanoparticles also prepared for control experiment. An overnight culture of bacteria and fungus cells was diluted to 106 CFU /mL (colony-forming unit per milliliter). Subsequently, 100 μL of these bacteria cell and fungus cell was added with or without the nanoparticle’s plate and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The number of colony forming units (CFU) was counted on each agar plate after the incubation, and the growth inhibition rate was calculated [37,38].

The prepared TiO2, Ag-TiO2, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (1 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (5 wt%) and TiO2@Pd/C materials were effectively utilized for Reactive Red 120 (RR 120) dye degradation under UV and solar light, and the results are shown in Fig. 1. Under UV A light, about 96% of degradation was achieved at 25 min for TiO2@Pd/C whereas bare TiO2 and Ag-TiO2 gave only 54% and 57% of degradations, respectively. For Pd/C loaded samples, almost all the samples gave 81% of degradation at the time of 25 min. The same reaction was performed under solar light, even at 75 min, the complete degradation was not achieved for all the samples. Under solar light, TiO2, Ag-TiO2 and TiO2 @Pd/C gave 46%, 53% and 76% of degradations, respectively at the time of 75 min. However, Ag with Pd/C loaded samples gave 92% of degradations at the same time. Although there was no significant enhancement in the photoactivity observed Ag with different Pd/C loaded samples, and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) had slightly higher activity at the time of 45 min. Hence, 3 wt% of Pd/C was found to be optimum in Ag-TiO2@Pd/C. Finally, the Ag and Pd/C modified samples were found to be more efficient compared to TiO2 for both processes. Photocatalytic activities trend in sunlight has changed when compared to UV. Overall, the ultraviolet process is found to be more efficient when compared to the solar process. Although, Ag modification did not show significant impact on the degradation of RR 120 dye in UV light, in the solar process, and in the anti-microbial activity, it impacts predominantly observed. UV–vis spectra of RR 120 (50 ppm) solution at different irradiation times (both UV and solar) with various catalyst are shown in Fig. 2. There is no significant change in UV–vis spectra during irradiation, but the intensities at 285 and 512 nm decreases gradually during degradation. This reveals that the intermediates do not absorb at analytical wavelengths of 285 and 512 nm.

It is very important to characterize the prepared materials, since, the characterization of the materials specifies the details about the surface properties, functional group, crystal nature and optical properties of the synthesized nanocomposites. The prepared Ag-TiO2, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (1 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (5 wt%) and TiO2@Pd/C were characterized using various characterization techniques. The surface functional group of the materials were analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and Raman spectroscopy. The crystalline nature of the composites was confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. The surface morphology of the prepared composites was studied by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) and field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) along with energy dispersive spectra (EDS). The oxidation state and binding energy of the components was confirmed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical properties of the materials were studied using diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and photoluminescence spectra (PL) measurements.

The surface functional group of the materials were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of the TiO2, Ag-TiO2, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (1 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (5 wt%) and TiO2@Pd/C were shown in Figs. S1a-S1f (see Supporting information’s). The Ti–O–Ti stretching vibrations of TiO2 was observed in the range between 500 and 750 cm−1 for all the samples [39,40]. The strong peak in the range between 3300 and 3500 cm−1 is ascribed to -OH stretching and the peak in the range between 1600 and 1650 cm−1 is attributed to -OH bending vibrations of surface hydroxyl group of the materials in all the cases [41]. It has been observed that there was no distinguishing difference between the spectra of TiO2 and Ag-TiO2 (Figs. S1a and S1b). However, there are several new peaks that appeared in the range between 1000 and 1450 cm−1 in all Pd/C loaded composites (Figs. S1c-S1f). The distinct peaks appeared in the range between 2900 and 2950 cm−1 in all Pd/C loaded composites, and this may be due to presence of carbon in the composites [42]. This study confirms the effective loading of Pd/C on Ag-TiO2.

By means of analysis of the Raman active modes of the semiconductors, we could get some important basic properties and structural parameters of the semiconducting nanocomposites. Fig. S2 shows the Raman spectra of TiO2 along with Ag and Pd/C modified samples. Four major peaks were observed at 140.7 (Eg (1)), 391.3 (Bg (1)), 523.8 (Ag (1) + Bg (2)) and 630.0 cm−1 (Eg (3)) in TiO2 sample, corresponding to different modes of anatase TiO2 (Fig. S2a). It has been noted that there is no difference in the Raman scattering patterns between Ag-TiO2@Pd/C composites and TiO2 (Figs. S2b-S2e). In Pd/C loaded samples, the peaks for Pd/C and Ag or its oxide phases were not observed [43], although, the characteristic G-band and D- band peaks of carbon materials obviously appear at 1599.8 and 1347.3 cm−1, respectively for Pd/C in TiO2-P25@Pd/C (9 wt%) [44]. The absence of these peaks in Pd/C loaded samples may be due to its low concentration.

X-ray diffractograms of TiO2, Ag-TiO2, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (1 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%), Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (5 wt%) and TiO2@Pd/C are given in Fig. S3. The 2ϴ values at 25.33° (101), 37.64° (004), 47.92° (200), 53.74° (105), 55.02° (211), 62.46° (204), 68.54° (116), 70.28° (220) and 75.38° (215) (JCPDS card no. 21-1272), correspond to anatase TiO2 (Fig. S3a) [17,45]. From the analysis of XRD pattern of Ag-TiO2, it is clear that in addition to anatase TiO2 peaks, there were two new peaks that appeared at 2ϴ values at 45.8° and 66.3° that correspond to (200) and (220) planes of cubic Ag, respectively (Fig. S3b) [46]. These two peaks reflected in all Ag loaded samples (Figs. S3c-S3e). Furthermore, the expected Pd and C peaks were not detected in XRD of Pd/C modified composites, and it may be due to less intense peaks or low in concentrations (Figs.S3c-S3f) [44]. Phase composition of the Ag and Pd/C modified composites did not change and hence retained as TiO2, and possibly loading of these (Ag and Pd in Pd/C) metals occurred rather than doping or substitution in the TiO2 lattice.

HR-TEM and FE-SEM have been conducted to give morphological explanation, composition, and crystal structure of TiO2 and silver and Pd/C modified TiO2 (Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%)) [44,47,48]. HR-TEM images of TiO2 and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) at different magnifications are shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S4 along with finger print region and SEAD pattern. From the HR-TEM analysis, the particles are in nm sizes and in the range between 10 and 40 nm for both cases. The particles are well separated and some of the particles appear to be round shaped in both cases. There is no visual difference between the images of TiO2 and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%). After the modification of Ag and Pd/C on TiO2, there was no morphological changes observed in HR-TEM-analysis. In order to vary this, the HR-TEM analysis of TiO2@Pd/C was performed and the images with various magnifications along with finger print and SAED pattern are shown in Fig. S5. As expected, the morphology of the particles was retained as TiO2. The particles are in nm size, and also identification of Pd/C in TiO2 was impossible in TiO2@Pd/C.

The morphology of the TiO2 and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) samples was further analyzed with FE-SEM analysis. Fig. 4 shows the FE-SEM images of TiO2 and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) samples at two different magnifications. Some morphological difference observed between TiO2 and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C samples. From Fig. 4a and b, the TiO2 has round or spherical shaped morphology. The TiO2 has good morphology and crystal structure. Although, identification between Ag and Pd/C on TiO2 was impossible at present, the surface of TiO2 was fully covered (see arrow marks) by Pd/C in Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) composite (Fig. 4c and d). The Ag and Pd/C were evenly distributed on TiO2 to form a homogenous mixture of Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) for effective environmental remediation. The presence of Ti, O, Ag, C and Pd in Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) composite is confirmed with EDS analysis and the EDS of Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) composite is shown as in Fig. S6.

XPS analysis was performed for Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) composite, and the results are given in Fig. 5. The survey spectrum (Fig. 5a) of the Ag-TiO2@Pd/C indicates peaks of elements Ti, O, Ag, Pd and C. Figs. 5b, c, d, e and f show the binding energy (B.E) peaks of Ti, O, Ag, Pd, and C, respectively. The Ti2p3/2 line observed at 457. 4 eV which is slight shift to lower binding energy (1.1 eV) when compared to pure TiO2 458.5 eV (Fig. 5b) [47]. The Ti 2p1/2 is observed at 463.2 eV, and these peaks are attributed to Ti4+ [47,49]. The slight peak shift of Ti2p3/2 line may be due to the presence of Ag and Pd/C in Ag-TiO2@Pd/C composite. The binding peak of O1s is observed at 528.6 eV and which is corresponding to lattice oxygen (OL) of TiO2 (Fig. 5c). In Fig. 5d, the metallic silver peaks at 366.6 and 372.2 eV are assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively [50,51]. The binding energy peaks of Pd is given in Fig. 5e. The binding energy peaks of Pd at 335.3 and 340.6 eV correspond to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of metallic palladium (Pd0), respectively [47,52]. In the C1s region (Fig. 5f), two peaks were observed at 283.6 and 287.5 eV which corresponds to various unit moieties of carbon such as C–H and C–O, respectively [53,54]. XPS results reveal that effective loading of Ag and Pd/C was on TiO2.

The photoluminescence intensity of the materials gave the information of rate of electron-hole recombination. PL spectra of TiO2, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) and TiO2@Pd/C nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 6. The TiO2 gave four predominant peaks at 363, 378, 467, and 538 nm. At the same time, the Pd loaded samples also gave the peaks at almost same regions with less intensity compared with TiO2. The less intense peaks may be due to presence of Ag or Pd in the composites, which can suppress the electron-hole recombination [44,55,56]. This may be the reason for the enchantment of photocatalytic activity of the metal loaded composites.

The solid-state UV-Vis (DRS) spectra of the materials are shown in Fig. S7. It can be seen that Ag-TiO2@Pd/C has slightly higher absorption than TiO2 in the entire visible region (>400 nm), and TiO2@Pd/C has higher absorbance than TiO2 in the UV region, and however, it has slightly less absorbance in visible region when compared with Ag-TiO2@Pd/C. Since TiO2@Pd/C has higher absorbance in UV region when compared with TiO2 and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C, this may be the reason for the higher photocatalytic activity of TiO2@Pd/C in UV-A light [57]. At the same time, the visible absorbance of TiO2@Pd/C is slightly less when compared with Ag-TiO2@Pd/C, and in visible region, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2@Pd/C is not significantly enhanced when compared with Ag-TiO2@Pd/C. The band gap energy (Eg) of the Ag-TiO2@Pd/C(3 wt%), TiO2@Pd/C and TiO2 was calculated through Tauc’s law from Kubelka–Munk function [43,58]. The band gap energies of TiO2, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C and TiO2@Pd/C were found to be 3.21, 3.25 and 3.31 eV, respectively (Fig. S8). After the metal modifications, the band gap energy of the materials was found to be slightly increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic study and effect of operational parameters
Effect of initial dye solution pH
ce:section.ce:para: The industrial effluents are usually coming with wide range of pH. It is very important to treat the effluent without altering the pH, hence, that could be a cost effective and energy efficient process for water remediation. In this point of view, the photodegradation of RR 120 was carried out in different initial pH with TiO2, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) and TiO2 @Pd/C under UV and solar light and the results are shown in Fig. 7. By analyzing the results of pH effect, it was observed that the degradation rate was highly influenced by the initial pH of the dye under both processes (UV and solar), and the rate of degradation increased up to 5 initially, and then slightly decreased. Almost the same trend was observed for all the catalysts. As observed earlier, UV process was found to be efficient than solar process. When compared with TiO2, the Ag and Pd/C modified samples show effective degradation in all the pH. The point of zero charge of TiO2 was reported to be 6.2 [59], and below this value TiO2 had positively charged surface, and RR 120 exists as negative ions at pH 7 [58], the electrostatic attraction between positively charged TiO2 and negatively charged dye solution may lead to strong interaction between the dye and catalyst. Thus, results in higher degradation [58].

Stability and reusability of the photocatalyst is an important parameter of the photocatalytic process because its use for a longer period of time leads to a significant cost reduction of the treatment of effluent. In the application point of view, the stability of the photocatalyst is a main property and this will make the photocatalyst used for industrial effluent treatment in a pilot or industrial scale level. Moreover, the use of direct sunlight for the degradation of industrial effluent is a cost-effective process, for this reason, the reusability of the TiO2@Pd/C and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) was tested on RR 120 dye degradation under both processes. The catalyst was separated by centrifugation at each run, washed with DI water and ethanol mixture and air dried in a hot air oven at 110 °C for 3 h, and reused. The results showed a drop in efficiency from 96% (first run) to 92% (fifth run) and 92% (first run) to 89% (fifth run) for UV and solar process, respectively ( Fig. 8).

The OFL degradation was performed with selective photocatalysts under solar light and the results are shown in Fig. S9. The TiO2 gave 75% of degradation at 90 min irradiation whereas Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) was used at the same condition about 87% of degradation observed. The UV spectra of OFL drug at different time of irradiation with TiO2 and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) are shown in Fig. 9. The time of irradiation increases, the intensity at 288 nm decreases gradually in both catalysts, and it also reveals that almost complete degradation of the OFL took place. There was no formation of new intermediates which absorb analytical wavelength at 288 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GC-MS analysis and degradation pathways of RR 120 and OFL
ce:section.ce:para: For photocatalytic degradation process, it is very important to analyze the intermediates formed during degradation as it is sometimes more hazardous than the starting material. The solution obtained (RR 120 and OFL) by different time of irradiation with Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) under solar process were subjected to GC-MS analysis. The obtained results were analyzed and the possible degradation pathways were proposed in Scheme 1 and Scheme 2, respectively for RR 120 and OFL. Based on the retention time and molecular weight obtained by GC-MS spectrum, a suitable degradation pathway is given for RR 120 by Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C (3 wt%) in Scheme 1, and corresponding data are given in Table 1.

Generally, the cleavage of azo linkage took place first with partial replacement of sulfonyl groups by hydroxyl groups in RR 120 dye, leading to the formation of compounds I, II and III. The compound I underwent further cleavage, produced degradation product (DPI) 8-((4-(buta-1,3-dien-2-ylamino)-6-chloro-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl) amino) naphthalene-1,3,6-triol and compound IV). Upon the repetitive attack of •OH, the degradation product DPI underwent further cleavage produced 4-((4-amino-6-chloro-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl) amino) naphthalen-2-ol (DP2) at the retention time of 39.465 min. The intermediate product DP 2 and compound IV undergo naphthalene ring cleavage and deamination and produced 4-chloro-N-phenyl-1,3,5-triazin-2-amine (DP3). At the same time, the intermediate compound IV produced one more C-N cleaved product 2,8-diaminonaphthalene-1,3,6-triol (DP4). The identified degradation product DP4 underwent partial deamination produced 8-aminonaphthalene-1,3,6-triol (DP5) which on further deamination produced naphthalene-1,3,6-triol (DP6). The degradation product 4-chloro-1,3,5-triazin-2-amine (DP7) was formed from DP3 via Ph-C-N cleavage. The intermediate compound II produced compound III via desulfonation. Finally, it is expected that the degradation products DP6, DP7 and intermediate compound III undergo further degradation to form mineralized CO2, water and mineral acids via this advanced oxidation process (AOPs) [60].

The photodegradation of OFL was started by the initial cleavage of several bonds (Scheme 2). This produced three decarboxylated degradation products 1-ethyl-8-hydroxy-7-(piperazin-1-yl) quinolin-4(1H)-one (DP1), 7-(dimethyl amino)-1-ethyl-6-fluoro-8-hydroxyquinolin-4(1H)-one (DPII) and 9-fluoro-3-methyl-2,3-dihydro-7H-[1,4]oxazino[2,3,4-ij]quinolin-7-one (DP III). The details of these identified intermediates are given in Table 2. The degradation product DP1 was formed via demethylation and C-F bond cleavage (defluorination) while DP2 and DP3 were formed via piperazine ring cleavage. The degradation product DP1 underwent Ph-C-N bond cleavage produced intermediate compound I and 1-ethyl-8-hydroxyquinolin-4(1H)-one (DP5). The degradation product DP2 underwent C-F bond cleavage (defluorination) and produced 7-(dimethyl amino)-1-ethyl-8-hydroxyquinolin-4(1H)-one (DP4) which further underwent Ph-C-N bond cleavage, and produced DP5. The degradation product DP3 underwent C-O bond cleavage, and formed 1-ethyl-6-fluoro-8-hydroxyquinolin-4(1H)-one (DP6). The degradation product DP5 underwent quinoline ring cleavage, and produced 2-(ethyl(methyl)amino)-3-hydroxybenzaldehyde (DP7) which on further underwent dihydroxylation, and produced 2-(ethyl(methyl) amino) benzaldehyde (DP8). At the same time, the degradation product DP6 also produced DP8 via the formation of DP5 and DP7. Lastly, these by-products compound II and DP8 are expected to be mineralized as CO2 and H2O.

The RR 120 degradation efficiency of the prepared composites was compared with other reported photocatalysts, the degradation of RR 120 [61–65] under different optimization conditions are listed in Table 3. Although each degradation processes have different optimization conditions, the comparison study gave the efficiency of the materials at particular reaction conditions. From Table 3, our prepared catalysts show a higher degradation efficiency in a shorter reaction time (Table 3, entries 8 & 9) under UV and direct solar light.

The mechanism of the degradation of RR 120 and OFL by Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) under solar light is shown in Scheme S2. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) edge potential of TiO2 was estimated via Mulliken electronegativity theory [17]. The three-component system (TiO2, Ag and Pd/C) is effectively involved in the degradation process. Upon irradiation, e-h pairs are generated in TiO2. Since TiO2 is a semiconductor and acts as photoactive center, the photo-excited electrons (CB) trapped by Ag particles react with dissolved oxygen and produce superoxide radical anions (O2 •–). The Fermi level of Ag is lower than CB position of anatase TiO2 and a Schottky barrier can form between the Ag and TiO2 interface, which could serve as an efficient electron trap, thus preventing photoexcited electron–hole recombination [66,67]. The Pd/C plays dual role in this process via adsorption (carbon) and electron trapping (palladium). Meanwhile hydroxyl radicals (•OH) was formed by the reaction between holes (VB) and water. Due to the presence of noble metals such as Ag and Pd, a greater number of electrons were trapped in the CB of TiO2, hence, results in the reduction of the electron-hole recombination significantly. Thus, enhances the photocatalytic activity of the material.

In order to find the most active species involved in the photodegradation process (solar) with Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%), the trapping experiment was performed and the results are shown in Fig. S10. Addition of 1 mmol of tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA, scavenger of •OH), potassium iodide (KI, scavenger of h+), benzoquinone (BQ, scavenger of O2 •–) and silver nitrate (AgNO3, scavenger of e–) contributes significantly to the decrease in the photodegradation efficiency of the catalyst (Eqs. 1–5) [68–72]. Without scavenger about 92% of degradation was observed at 75 min. At the same time addition of TBA, KI, BQ and AgNO3 gave 27.6, 23.9, 17.8 and 14.8 percentages of degradation, respectively. Among the scavengers used, addition of AgNO3 significantly reduced the catalytic activity and reveals that electrons (e–) are the main active species in this degradation. The active species contributes the following order e−>O2 •–> h+>•OH.

At present, microbial infection has been a global challenge in worldwide. The prepared nanocomposites have been effectively utilized for biological applications. The anti-microbial activity of the Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) and TiO2@Pd/C was tested against twenty pathogens (eighteen bacterial and two fungi) and the results are shown in Table 4 and Table 5, respectively, along with Ag-TiO2 (Table S1) and TiO2 (Table S2) results for comparison. Anti-microbial activity was evaluated by growth determination (%) (growth reduction assay) vs various concentration of sample (0.78, 1.56, 3.12, 6.25, and 12.5 µg/mL). From the tables, Ag-TiO2 @Pd/C was found to be very efficient for most of the pathogens when compared with other composites and TiO2. Almost no growth was observed with Ag-TiO2@Pd/C against Staphylococcus aureus (GPC) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (GNB) at the concentration of 3.12 µg/mL. Besides, no growth was observed against Bacillus subtilis (GPB), and Escherichia coli (GNB) at the concentration of 6.25 µg/mL (Table 4).

When TiO2@Pd/C was tested against Staphylococcus aureus and Bacillus subtilis about 42% and 26% of growth was observed, respectively even at higher concentration 12.5 µg/mL (Table 5). Under the same conditions, Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa gave 23% and 26% of growth (Table 5). Moreover, a significant growth was observed against above tested pathogens with Ag-TiO2 and TiO2 even at higher concentration (Tables S1 and S2). Among the tested bacterial pathogens, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C (3 wt%) completely controlled the growth of Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and significantly controlled the growth of Micrococcus sp., Enterobacter sp., Flavobacterium sp., Klebsiella pneumoniae, Proteus mirabilis, Proteus vulgaris, Salmonella typhi, Salmonella typhimurium, Serratia marcescens, Vibrio cholerae and moderately controlled the growth of Acinetobacter baumanii, Providentia sp., Salmonella paratyphi A, and Salmonella paratyphi B. These results reveal that Ag-TiO2@Pd/C was found to be efficient when compared with other prepared composites towards anti-microbial activity.

The nanocomposite Ag-TiO2@Pd/C was also tested against fungal pathogens such as Candida albicans and Cryptococcus neoformans (Table 4). Ag-TiO2@Pd/C efficiently controlled the growth of Candida albicans than Cryptococcus neoformans, and at 12.5 µg/mL concentration about 19% and 43% of growth was observed, respectively. Under the same condition, TiO2@Pd/C gave 40% and 78% of growth against Candida albicans and Cryptococcus neoformans, respectively. The higher activity of Ag-TiO2@Pd/C may be due to presence of noble metals such as Ag and Pd, that would have caused the efficient generation of surface oxygen species which are responsible for the control of the growth of pathogens. The growth control effect is depending on the type and concentration of the materials. Moreover, it is expected that there is an interaction between the nanocomposites and cell wall, and may cause membrane damage [73,74].

Ag-TiO2@Pd/C nanocomposites were prepared by two step process such as sol-gel cum solid-state dispersion method for energy efficient environmental remediation. The prepared nanocomposites characterized using various characterization techniques such as FT-IR, Raman, XRD, HR-TEM, FE-SEM, EDS, XPS, PL and DRS. Metallic silver (Ag0) in Ag-TiO2@Pd/C was confirmed by XPS measurements. The Ag-TiO2@Pd/C effectively degrade the RR 120 under solar light, although its efficiency is somewhat less in UV-A light when compared with TiO2@Pd/C for RR 120 degradation. The influence of initial dye pH on degradation was studied with selected catalyst under UV and solar light. The reusability of TiO2@Pd/C and Ag-TiO2@Pd/C was tested for RR 120 degradation under UV and solar light, respectively and it was found that both composites were stable and reusable. The photo activity of the Ag-TiO2@Pd/C was further confirmed with OFL degradation under direct sunlight. The intermediates formed during degradation of RR 120 and OFL with Ag-TiO2@Pd/C under solar light were analyzed by GC-MS. From the GC-MS, seven (DP1-DP7) and eight (DP1-DP8) intermediates were found for RR 120 and OFL, respectively. Among the scavengers, addition of AgNO3 significantly reduces the catalytic activity and reveals that electrons (e–) are the main active species in this degradation. The active species contributes the following order e−> O2 •–> h+>•OH. The prepared nanocomposites were effectively utilized for anti-microbial activity against twenty bacterial and fungal pathogens. Among the tested bacterial pathogens, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C completely controlled the growth of Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus subtilis, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Moreover, Ag-TiO2@Pd/C was found to be very efficient towards anti-microbial activity when compared with other nanocomposites. It is also expected that the prepared nanocomposites could be effectively utilized for energy and environmental application.
